title: Bronze & Alloys
subtitle: Copper and tin, in the right measure

pages.text: An alloy is a recipe of metals
Bronze is no ore — you &6alloy&r it. Charge a crucible with both &6copper&r and &6tin&r and melt them together. There is no bronze vein to mine; the metal is made in the melt.

The proportions matter. A clean bronze needs &6copper 60–90%&r and &6tin 10–40%&r of the charge. Hit that band and the whole melt resolves to Molten Bronze. Stray too far — too little tin, or a third metal in the pot — and it falls back to a poorer, off-ratio result. The Bloomery readout shows the running composition and a ✓ / ✗ ratio check as you charge.
Bronze pours into the same moulds as copper and tin, but casts a harder, longer-lasting tool. It is the backbone of the age: better than copper, and the proving ground before iron.
